answer:
Feature Flags — Compare Two Approaches

Core Definition

Feature flags (feature toggles) are conditional switches in code that enable or disable features without deploying new code. They allow gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and quick kill-switches for problematic features. Two approaches: config-based (JSON/env vars) versus service-based (dedicated flag management like LaunchDarkly or custom service).

Manoj's Project Context

For the HR Leave Management System, Manoj compared config-based flags (environment variables checked at startup) versus a database-backed flag service (flags read from database, changeable without redeploy) when rolling out the new AI leave assistant feature to managers first, then all employees.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// Approach A: Config-based (simple env var check)
const FEATURES = {
  aiAssistant: process.env.FEATURE_AI_ASSISTANT === 'true',
  darkMode: process.env.FEATURE_DARK_MODE === 'true',
  newDashboard: process.env.FEATURE_NEW_DASHBOARD === 'true',
};

if (FEATURES.aiAssistant) {
  app.register(import('./routes/ai-assistant'));
}

// Approach B: Database-backed flag service (HR Leave System choice)
interface FeatureFlag {
  name: string;
  enabled: boolean;
  targetRoles?: string[];    // Only these roles see the feature
  rolloutPercent?: number;   // 0-100 for gradual rollout
}

class FeatureFlagService {
  private cache = new Map<string, FeatureFlag>();
  private cacheExpiry = 0;
  private readonly CACHE_TTL = 60_000; // 1 minute

  async isEnabled(flagName: string, user?: { id: string; role: string }): Promise<boolean> {
    await this.refreshCache();
    const flag = this.cache.get(flagName);
    if (!flag || !flag.enabled) return false;

    // Role-based targeting
    if (flag.targetRoles?.length && user) {
      if (!flag.targetRoles.includes(user.role)) return false;
    }

    // Percentage rollout (consistent per user using hash)
    if (flag.rolloutPercent !== undefined && flag.rolloutPercent < 100 && user) {
      const hash = hashCode(`${flagName}:${user.id}`) % 100;
      if (hash >= flag.rolloutPercent) return false;
    }

    return true;
  }

  private async refreshCache() {
    if (Date.now() < this.cacheExpiry) return;
    const flags = await prisma.featureFlag.findMany();
    this.cache.clear();
    flags.forEach(f => this.cache.set(f.name, f));
    this.cacheExpiry = Date.now() + this.CACHE_TTL;
  }
}

// Usage: Gradual rollout of AI assistant
// Phase 1: managers only
await prisma.featureFlag.create({
  data: { name: 'ai-assistant', enabled: true, targetRoles: ['manager', 'admin'], rolloutPercent: 100 },
});

// Phase 2: 50% of employees
await prisma.featureFlag.update({
  where: { name: 'ai-assistant' },
  data: { targetRoles: ['employee', 'manager', 'admin'], rolloutPercent: 50 },
});

// Phase 3: everyone
await prisma.featureFlag.update({
  where: { name: 'ai-assistant' },
  data: { rolloutPercent: 100 },
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

When rolling out the AI assistant, Manoj initially used env vars (`FEATURE_AI_ASSISTANT=true`). But he needed to enable it for managers first, then gradually for employees. Env vars couldn't do per-role targeting. He built a database-backed flag service with role-based targeting and percentage rollouts. A consistent hash ensures the same 50% of users always see the feature (no flickering). The 1-minute cache TTL means flag changes take effect within a minute without redeploy. Phase 1 (managers) caught a bug in the AI assistant before it reached employees.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Stale flags never cleaned upTrack flag creation date; remove flags older than 30 days post-launch
Cache serves stale flag valueKeep TTL short (1 min); provide cache-bust endpoint for emergencies
Percentage rollout inconsistent across requestsHash user ID + flag name for deterministic bucket assignment

How to Validate

  • Enable flag for managers only — verify employees don't see feature
  • Set 50% rollout — verify consistent users see/don't see feature
  • Disable flag — verify feature disappears within cache TTL
  • Change flag in DB — verify no redeploy needed

Feature Flags — Design Implementation

Core Definition

Designing a feature flag system involves creating a flag storage mechanism, evaluation logic with targeting rules, a management UI or API, and integration points in both backend and frontend code. The system should support gradual rollouts, kill-switches, and flag lifecycle management.

Manoj's Project Context

For ContactsHub, Manoj designed a lightweight feature flag system stored in MongoDB. He needed to roll out a new AI-powered contact search feature to beta testers first, then progressively to all users, with a kill-switch if the AI API had issues.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// Feature flag schema (MongoDB)
const flagSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  name: { type: String, unique: true, required: true },
  description: String,
  enabled: { type: Boolean, default: false },
  rules: [{
    type: { type: String, enum: ['role', 'userId', 'percent'] },
    values: [String],  // roles or user IDs
    percent: Number,   // for percentage rollout
  }],
  createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now },
  updatedAt: Date,
});

// API for managing flags
app.get('/api/admin/flags', requirePermission('flags:read'), async (req, res) => {
  const flags = await Flag.find().sort({ name: 1 });
  res.json(flags);
});

app.put('/api/admin/flags/:name', requirePermission('flags:update'), async (req, res) => {
  const flag = await Flag.findOneAndUpdate(
    { name: req.params.name },
    { ...req.body, updatedAt: new Date() },
    { upsert: true, new: true }
  );
  flagCache.invalidate(flag.name); // Bust cache immediately
  res.json(flag);
});

// Frontend: Expose flags via API endpoint
app.get('/api/flags', async (req, res) => {
  // Only return flag states relevant to this user
  const flags = await Flag.find({ enabled: true });
  const evaluated = {};
  for (const flag of flags) {
    evaluated[flag.name] = await featureFlags.isEnabled(flag.name, req.user);
  }
  res.json(evaluated);
});

// React hook for frontend flag checking
function useFeatureFlag(flagName: string): boolean {
  const { data } = useSWR('/api/flags', fetcher, {
    refreshInterval: 60_000, // Re-check every 60s
  });
  return data?.[flagName] ?? false;
}

// Usage in React component
function ContactSearch() {
  const aiSearchEnabled = useFeatureFlag('ai-search');

  return (
    <div>
      {aiSearchEnabled ? (
        <AISearchBar />
      ) : (
        <BasicSearchBar />
      )}
    </div>
  );
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

ContactsHub needed to test the new AI search with beta users before full rollout. Manoj created a flag `ai-search` with a rule targeting specific user IDs (beta testers). When the AI API had a brief outage during testing, he toggled the flag off via the admin API — instant kill-switch without redeploying. The cache invalidation on update ensured the flag change took effect within seconds. On the frontend, the `useFeatureFlag` hook re-checks every 60 seconds, so users saw the feature disappear within a minute of the kill-switch. After fixing the AI issue, he toggled it back on and expanded to 25% of all users via percentage rollout.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Old flags accumulate as tech debtAdd createdAt tracking; review and remove flags quarterly
Flag evaluation adds latencyCache flags in memory; evaluate locally without DB call per request
Frontend flicker when flag state changesUse loading state; default to old behavior until flags load

How to Validate

  • Create flag for beta users — verify only they see the feature
  • Toggle flag off — verify feature disappears within 60 seconds
  • Set 25% rollout — verify consistent subset sees feature
  • Check admin API — verify CRUD operations work correctly

CSV Import Pipeline — Compare Two Approaches

Core Definition

A CSV import pipeline reads, validates, transforms, and loads data from CSV files into a database. Two approaches: load-all-at-once (read entire file into memory, validate, bulk insert) versus streaming pipeline (read line by line, validate each row, insert in batches).

Manoj's Project Context

For ContactsHub, Manoj compared both approaches for importing contacts from CSV. Small files (100 contacts) worked fine with load-all, but a 50,000-contact CSV from a CRM export crashed the server with an out-of-memory error.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

import { createReadStream } from 'fs';
import { parse } from 'csv-parse';
import { z } from 'zod';

// Validation schema
const ContactRow = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1),
  email: z.string().email(),
  phone: z.string().optional(),
  city: z.string().optional(),
  department: z.string().optional(),
});

// Approach B: Streaming pipeline (ContactsHub choice)
async function importContactsStream(
  filePath: string,
  onProgress: (processed: number, errors: number) => void
): Promise<{ imported: number; errors: { row: number; error: string }[] }> {
  const errors: { row: number; error: string }[] = [];
  let batch: any[] = [];
  let imported = 0;
  let rowNum = 0;
  const BATCH_SIZE = 100;

  const parser = createReadStream(filePath)
    .pipe(parse({ columns: true, trim: true, skip_empty_lines: true }));

  for await (const row of parser) {
    rowNum++;

    // Validate each row
    const result = ContactRow.safeParse(row);
    if (!result.success) {
      errors.push({ row: rowNum, error: result.error.issues[0].message });
      continue;
    }

    batch.push({
      ...result.data,
      createdAt: new Date(),
      importedFrom: 'csv',
    });

    // Insert in batches
    if (batch.length >= BATCH_SIZE) {
      await db.contacts.insertMany(batch);
      imported += batch.length;
      batch = [];
      onProgress(imported, errors.length);
    }
  }

  // Insert remaining
  if (batch.length > 0) {
    await db.contacts.insertMany(batch);
    imported += batch.length;
  }

  return { imported, errors };
}

// API endpoint with file upload
app.post('/api/contacts/import', upload.single('file'), async (req, res) => {
  if (!req.file) return res.status(400).json({ error: 'No file uploaded' });

  // Respond immediately, process in background
  const jobId = crypto.randomUUID();
  res.status(202).json({ jobId, status: 'processing' });

  const result = await importContactsStream(
    req.file.path,
    (processed, errors) => {
      // Update job progress in database
      db.importJobs.updateOne({ _id: jobId }, { $set: { processed, errors } });
    }
  );

  await db.importJobs.updateOne({ _id: jobId }, {
    $set: { status: 'completed', ...result },
  });
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

A user uploaded a 50,000-row CSV export from their CRM. The load-all approach read the entire 15MB file into memory, parsed all rows (creating 50K objects), then tried to insert them all at once. The server ran out of memory and crashed. Manoj switched to streaming: the CSV is read line-by-line, validated with Zod per row, and inserted in batches of 100. Memory usage stays constant at ~10MB regardless of file size. He also added per-row error tracking so the user sees exactly which rows failed validation, and a progress callback so the frontend can show a progress bar.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Large CSV causes OOMUse streaming parser; process in batches
Partial import with no rollbackTrack import batch ID; provide "undo import" that deletes by batch ID
No feedback on import progressEmit progress per batch; store in DB for polling

How to Validate

  • Import small CSV (100 rows) — verify all contacts created
  • Import large CSV (50K rows) — verify memory stays under 50MB
  • Import CSV with invalid rows — verify errors reported per row
  • Check progress endpoint during import — verify real-time updates

CSV Import Pipeline — Design Implementation

Core Definition

Designing a CSV import pipeline involves handling file upload, parsing, validation, transformation, deduplication, batch insertion, error reporting, and rollback. A production-ready pipeline also handles encoding issues, column mapping, and provides import history.

Manoj's Project Context

For the HR Leave Management System, Manoj designed a CSV import pipeline for bulk employee onboarding. HR uploads a CSV with employee details (name, email, department, manager), and the system creates accounts, assigns roles, and sends welcome emails — all in a tracked import job.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// Employee import pipeline — complete implementation
import { parse } from 'csv-parse';
import { z } from 'zod';
import { createReadStream } from 'fs';

const EmployeeRow = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(2),
  email: z.string().email(),
  department: z.string().min(1),
  role: z.enum(['employee', 'manager']),
  managerId: z.string().optional(),
});

interface ImportResult {
  total: number;
  created: number;
  updated: number;
  failed: number;
  errors: { row: number; field: string; message: string }[];
}

async function importEmployees(filePath: string): Promise<ImportResult> {
  const result: ImportResult = { total: 0, created: 0, updated: 0, failed: 0, errors: [] };
  let batch: any[] = [];
  let rowNum = 0;

  const parser = createReadStream(filePath)
    .pipe(parse({ columns: true, trim: true, bom: true })); // bom: handle Excel UTF-8 BOM

  for await (const row of parser) {
    rowNum++;
    result.total++;

    // Validate
    const validation = EmployeeRow.safeParse(row);
    if (!validation.success) {
      result.failed++;
      validation.error.issues.forEach(issue => {
        result.errors.push({ row: rowNum, field: issue.path.join('.'), message: issue.message });
      });
      continue;
    }

    batch.push(validation.data);

    if (batch.length >= 50) {
      await processBatch(batch, result);
      batch = [];
    }
  }

  if (batch.length > 0) await processBatch(batch, result);
  return result;
}

async function processBatch(employees: z.infer<typeof EmployeeRow>[], result: ImportResult) {
  for (const emp of employees) {
    // Deduplication: check if email already exists
    const existing = await prisma.employee.findUnique({ where: { email: emp.email } });

    if (existing) {
      // Update existing employee
      await prisma.employee.update({ where: { email: emp.email }, data: emp });
      result.updated++;
    } else {
      // Create new employee with temporary password
      const tempPassword = crypto.randomBytes(8).toString('hex');
      await prisma.employee.create({
        data: { ...emp, password: await bcrypt.hash(tempPassword, 10) },
      });

      // Queue welcome email
      await jobQueue.enqueue('send-welcome-email', { email: emp.email, tempPassword });
      result.created++;
    }
  }
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

HR uploaded a CSV exported from Excel with 50 new employees. The import failed on row 1 because Excel's CSV export includes a BOM (Byte Order Mark) that corrupted the first header column name. Manoj added `bom: true` to the CSV parser config to handle this. Another issue: 3 employees had emails that already existed — instead of failing, Manoj added deduplication logic to update existing records. The final import report showed "45 created, 3 updated, 2 failed (invalid email format)" with a downloadable error CSV showing exactly which rows failed and why.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Excel BOM corrupts first columnEnable BOM handling in CSV parser
Duplicates cause unique constraint errorsCheck for existing records; upsert or report as update
No way to undo bulk importTag imported records with batch ID; provide rollback by batch

How to Validate

  • Import CSV with valid + invalid rows — verify correct counts
  • Import CSV with duplicates — verify updates instead of errors
  • Import Excel-exported CSV — verify BOM handled correctly
  • Download error report — verify row numbers and messages are accurate

Notification Service — Design Implementation

Core Definition

A notification service manages sending messages to users across multiple channels (email, in-app, push, SMS). It handles template rendering, delivery preferences, rate limiting, and delivery tracking. A well-designed service decouples notification logic from business logic.

Manoj's Project Context

For the HR Leave Management System, Manoj designed a notification service that sends leave status updates via email and in-app notifications. Managers receive approval requests, employees receive status updates, and admins receive daily digest summaries.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// notification-service.ts
type Channel = 'email' | 'inApp';
type TemplateId = 'leave_submitted' | 'leave_approved' | 'leave_rejected' | 'approval_request';

interface NotificationPayload {
  templateId: TemplateId;
  recipientId: string;
  data: Record<string, string>;
  channels?: Channel[]; // Override default channels
}

const TEMPLATES: Record<TemplateId, { subject: string; body: string }> = {
  leave_approved: {
    subject: 'Leave Approved — {{leaveType}}',
    body: 'Hi {{employeeName}}, your {{leaveType}} leave from {{startDate}} to {{endDate}} has been approved by {{managerName}}.',
  },
  approval_request: {
    subject: 'Leave Approval Needed — {{employeeName}}',
    body: '{{employeeName}} has requested {{leaveType}} leave from {{startDate}} to {{endDate}}. Please review.',
  },
  // ... other templates
};

class NotificationService {
  async send(payload: NotificationPayload): Promise<void> {
    const recipient = await prisma.employee.findUnique({ where: { id: payload.recipientId } });
    if (!recipient) return;

    // Check user preferences
    const prefs = await this.getPreferences(payload.recipientId);
    const channels = payload.channels ?? this.getDefaultChannels(prefs);

    // Rate limiting
    if (await this.isRateLimited(payload.recipientId)) {
      console.warn(`Rate limited: skipping notification for ${payload.recipientId}`);
      return;
    }

    // Render template
    const template = TEMPLATES[payload.templateId];
    const rendered = {
      subject: this.render(template.subject, payload.data),
      body: this.render(template.body, payload.data),
    };

    // Send via each channel
    for (const channel of channels) {
      try {
        if (channel === 'email' && prefs.email) {
          await jobQueue.enqueue('send-email', {
            to: recipient.email,
            subject: rendered.subject,
            body: rendered.body,
          });
        } else if (channel === 'inApp') {
          await prisma.notification.create({
            data: {
              recipientId: payload.recipientId,
              title: rendered.subject,
              body: rendered.body,
              read: false,
            },
          });
          // Push via WebSocket if user is online
          socketServer.to(payload.recipientId).emit('notification', rendered);
        }

        await this.trackDelivery(payload, channel, 'sent');
      } catch (err) {
        await this.trackDelivery(payload, channel, 'failed');
      }
    }
  }

  private render(template: string, data: Record<string, string>): string {
    return template.replace(/\{\{(\w+)\}\}/g, (_, key) => data[key] ?? '');
  }
}

// Usage in leave approval flow
const notifications = new NotificationService();

app.post('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (req, res) => {
  const leave = await prisma.leave.update({
    where: { id: req.params.id },
    data: { status: 'approved' },
    include: { employee: true },
  });

  await notifications.send({
    templateId: 'leave_approved',
    recipientId: leave.employeeId,
    data: {
      employeeName: leave.employee.name,
      leaveType: leave.leaveType,
      startDate: leave.startDate,
      endDate: leave.endDate,
      managerName: req.user.name,
    },
  });

  res.json(leave);
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

The HR system initially sent emails directly from route handlers — duplicated across 8 different leave actions. Manoj centralized all notifications into a NotificationService with template rendering, channel routing, and delivery tracking. When a manager complained about receiving 15 notifications in an hour (from a bulk import of leave requests), he added rate limiting: max 10 notifications per hour per user, with excess queued for the next window. He also added user preferences so managers can opt into daily digest mode instead of real-time notifications.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Notification logic duplicated across routesCentralize in a NotificationService; routes just call send()
Notification spam from bulk operationsImplement rate limiting; offer digest mode for high-volume users
No tracking of delivery failuresLog delivery status (sent/failed) per notification per channel

How to Validate

  • Approve leave — verify email AND in-app notification sent
  • Disable email in preferences — verify only in-app notification sent
  • Trigger 15 notifications — verify rate limiter caps at 10
  • Check delivery tracking — verify all sent/failed statuses recorded

Notification Service — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

Notification service debugging involves diagnosing delivery failures: emails ending up in spam, WebSocket notifications not reaching offline users, template rendering errors, and notification preferences not being respected.

Manoj's Project Context

In ContactsHub, Manoj debugged an issue where in-app notifications worked for online users but were lost for users who were offline when the notification was sent. The WebSocket emit silently failed, and there was no fallback persistence.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// BEFORE: WebSocket-only notification (lost for offline users)
async function notifyUser(userId: string, message: string) {
  socketServer.to(userId).emit('notification', { message });
  // If user is offline, this silently does nothing!
}

// AFTER: Persist-first, push-second pattern
async function notifyUser(userId: string, notification: {
  title: string;
  body: string;
  link?: string;
}) {
  // Step 1: ALWAYS persist to database
  const saved = await db.notifications.insertOne({
    userId,
    ...notification,
    read: false,
    createdAt: new Date(),
  });

  // Step 2: Push via WebSocket if online (best-effort)
  const isOnline = socketServer.sockets.adapter.rooms.has(userId);
  if (isOnline) {
    socketServer.to(userId).emit('notification', {
      id: saved.insertedId,
      ...notification,
    });
  }
  // If offline, they'll see it when they load unread notifications
}

// Frontend: Load unread notifications on connect
const socket = io(API_URL);

socket.on('connect', async () => {
  // Fetch any notifications missed while offline
  const unread = await fetch('/api/notifications/unread').then(r => r.json());
  unread.forEach(n => showNotificationBadge(n));
});

// Listen for real-time notifications
socket.on('notification', (notification) => {
  showNotificationBadge(notification);
});

// API: Get unread notifications
app.get('/api/notifications/unread', async (req, res) => {
  const notifications = await db.notifications
    .find({ userId: req.user.id, read: false })
    .sort({ createdAt: -1 })
    .limit(20)
    .toArray();
  res.json(notifications);
});

// API: Mark as read
app.patch('/api/notifications/:id/read', async (req, res) => {
  await db.notifications.updateOne(
    { _id: new ObjectId(req.params.id), userId: req.user.id },
    { $set: { read: true, readAt: new Date() } }
  );
  res.json({ success: true });
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

ContactsHub users reported missing notifications about shared contacts. The notification system only used WebSocket emit — if the user's browser was closed, the notification was lost forever. Manoj implemented the "persist-first, push-second" pattern: every notification is saved to MongoDB first, then pushed via WebSocket as a bonus for online users. When users reconnect, the frontend fetches unread notifications from the API. He also added a notification badge count that polls every 60 seconds as a fallback for WebSocket connection drops. The fix ensured zero notification loss regardless of user connectivity.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
WebSocket-only notifications lost for offline usersPersist to DB first; WebSocket is best-effort delivery
Unread count grows unboundedAuto-mark old notifications as read after 30 days; paginate
Duplicate notifications on reconnect + real-timeDeduplicate by notification ID on the frontend

How to Validate

  • Send notification while user is offline — verify it appears on next login
  • Send notification while user is online — verify real-time delivery via WebSocket
  • Check unread count — verify badge shows correct number
  • Mark notification as read — verify it disappears from unread list
